**James Barrie Sikking** (March 5, 1934 – July 13, 2024) was an American actor, best known for his roles as Lt. Howard Hunter on the 1980s television series *[Hill Street Blues](/wiki/Hill_Street_Blues "Hill Street Blues")* and Dr. David Howser on *[Doogie Howser, M.D.](/wiki/Doogie_Howser,_M.D. "Doogie Howser, M.D.")*[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) His career spanned six decades.[[3]](#cite_note-Nungesser-3)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_B._Sikking&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

James Barrie Sikking was born in Los Angeles on March 5, 1934, to Arthur and Sue (née Paxton) Sikking. Sikking was the youngest of five children.[[4]](#cite_note-Barnes-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Zee-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Garner-6)[[7]](#cite_note-CST-7) His mother co-founded [Santa Monica, California](/wiki/Santa_Monica,_California "Santa Monica, California")'s Unity-by-the-Sea Church. He attended [El Segundo High School](/wiki/El_Segundo_High_School "El Segundo High School").[[8]](#cite_note-people-8) Sikking graduated from [University of California, Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles") in 1959.[[9]](#cite_note-9) During his time at UCLA, he worked at the Central Stage, the production program at UCLA and [Los Angeles County](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County "Los Angeles County") [lifeguard](/wiki/Lifeguard "Lifeguard") to pay for college.[[10]](#cite_note-Buck-10)

While in college during the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), Sikking served in the [U.S. Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army").[[8]](#cite_note-people-8) He stated he based his approach to his *[Hill Street Blues](/wiki/Hill_Street_Blues "Hill Street Blues")* character on one of his [drill sergeants](/wiki/Drill_instructor "Drill instructor") from [basic training](/wiki/Military_recruit_training "Military recruit training") at [Fort Bragg](/wiki/Fort_Bragg "Fort Bragg").[[4]](#cite_note-Barnes-4)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_B._Sikking&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_B._Sikking&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Films")]

Sikking's first film was in [Roger Corman](/wiki/Roger_Corman "Roger Corman")'s *[Five Guns West](/wiki/Five_Guns_West "Five Guns West")* (1955) as a Union Sergeant [uncredited](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance").[[7]](#cite_note-CST-7)[[11]](#cite_note-Pryor-11) He was in *[The Strangler](/wiki/The_Strangler "The Strangler")* (1964),[[12]](#cite_note-Archer-12) *[The Carpetbaggers](/wiki/The_Carpetbaggers_(film) "The Carpetbaggers (film)")* (1964),[[13]](#cite_note-Crowther-13) *[Von Ryan's Express](/wiki/Von_Ryan%27s_Express "Von Ryan's Express")* (1965),[[14]](#cite_note-Variety-14) *[In Like Flint](/wiki/In_Like_Flint "In Like Flint")* (1967),[[15]](#cite_note-Bosley-15) *[Point Blank](/wiki/Point_Blank_(1967_film) "Point Blank (1967 film)")* (1967),[[16]](#cite_note-Ebert-16) the [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley") film *[Charro!](/wiki/Charro! "Charro!")* (1969),[[5]](#cite_note-Zee-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Garner-6) and *[Daddy's Gone A-Hunting](/wiki/Daddy%27s_Gone_A-Hunting_(1969_film) "Daddy's Gone A-Hunting (1969 film)")* (1969).[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaltin2014312-17)

In the 1970s, Sikking was in *[Escape from the Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Escape_from_the_Planet_of_the_Apes "Escape from the Planet of the Apes")* in an uncredited role (1971),[[18]](#cite_note-Greenspun-18) *[Brother on the Run](/w/index.php?title=Brother_on_the_Run&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brother on the Run (page does not exist)")* (1973),[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWalkerRauschWatson2009198-19) *[Capricorn One](/wiki/Capricorn_One "Capricorn One")* (1978),[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMavis201548-20) and *[The Electric Horseman](/wiki/The_Electric_Horseman "The Electric Horseman")* (1979).[[21]](#cite_note-Crosby-21) In the 1980s, he was in *[The Star Chamber](/wiki/The_Star_Chamber "The Star Chamber")* (1983),[[22]](#cite_note-Boyar-22) *[Up the Creek](/wiki/Up_the_Creek_(1984_film) "Up the Creek (1984 film)")* (1984),[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENowlan2025610-23) *[Star Trek III: The Search for Spock](/wiki/Star_Trek_III:_The_Search_for_Spock "Star Trek III: The Search for Spock")* (1984),[[24]](#cite_note-Britt-24)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESenn202438-25) and *[Soul Man](/wiki/Soul_Man_(film) "Soul Man (film)")* (1986).[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENowlan2025333-26)

In the 1990s, Sikking was in the remake *[Narrow Margin](/wiki/Narrow_Margin "Narrow Margin")* (1990) of 1956's *[The Narrow Margin](/wiki/The_Narrow_Margin "The Narrow Margin")*,[[10]](#cite_note-Buck-10)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFreese2017230–231-27)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirsch2004360-28) *[Final Approach](/wiki/Final_Approach_(1991_film) "Final Approach (1991 film)")* (1991),[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaltin2017461-29)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarsanti201480-30) *[The Pelican Brief](/wiki/The_Pelican_Brief_(film) "The Pelican Brief (film)")* (1993),[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERyan202411–12-31) and *[Whisper of the Heart](/wiki/Whisper_of_the_Heart "Whisper of the Heart")* (1995).[[32]](#cite_note-Disney-32) In the 2000s he was in *[Made of Honor](/wiki/Made_of_Honor "Made of Honor")* (2008).[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaltin2008853-33)

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_B._Sikking&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Television")]

In the 1960s, Sikking was in a number of television series such as *[Assignment: Underwater](/wiki/Assignment:_Underwater "Assignment: Underwater")*,[[4]](#cite_note-Barnes-4) *[Honey West](/wiki/Honey_West "Honey West")*,[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWagner2011233-34) and *[Hogan's Heroes](/wiki/Hogan%27s_Heroes "Hogan's Heroes")*.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoyce1998202-35)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoyce1998206-36)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoyce1998233-37) From 1971–76, he played Jim Hobart, an alcoholic surgeon, on the [ABC Daytime](/wiki/ABC_Daytime "ABC Daytime") [soap opera](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera") *[General Hospital](/wiki/General_Hospital "General Hospital")*.[[21]](#cite_note-Crosby-21)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerrace1985162-38) In the 1970s, Sikking was in *[M\*A\*S\*H](/wiki/M*A*S*H_(TV_series) "M*A*S*H (TV series)")*,[[39]](#cite_note-Bentley-39) *[Rich Man, Poor Man Book II](/wiki/Rich_Man,_Poor_Man_Book_II "Rich Man, Poor Man Book II")* (1976) [miniseries](/wiki/Miniseries "Miniseries"),[[40]](#cite_note-Brennan-40) and portrayed Geoffrey St. James on the [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") comedy series *[Turnabout](/wiki/Turnabout_(TV_series) "Turnabout (TV series)")*.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerrance20111113-41)

Sikking appeared as Lieutenant (later demoted to Sergeant) Howard Hunter on the TV series *[Hill Street Blues](/wiki/Hill_Street_Blues "Hill Street Blues")* from 1981 to 1987.[[7]](#cite_note-CST-7) Sikking's performance earned him his first Emmy nomination in 1984.[[42]](#cite_note-Russell-42)[[3]](#cite_note-Nungesser-3)[[7]](#cite_note-CST-7) In 1989, *Hill Street Blues'* creator, [Steven Bochco](/wiki/Steven_Bochco "Steven Bochco"), cast Sikking in the [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") television series *[Doogie Howser, M.D.](/wiki/Doogie_Howser,_M.D. "Doogie Howser, M.D.")* as Dr. David Howser,[[7]](#cite_note-CST-7)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerrance2011278-43) father of the title character. On the 1997 drama series *[Brooklyn South](/wiki/Brooklyn_South "Brooklyn South")* he portrayed Captain Stan Jonas, for which he won a [People's Choice Award](/wiki/People%27s_Choice_Award "People's Choice Award") in 1998.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerrace2011139–140-44) Sikking was in quite a few [made-for-TV movies](/wiki/Television_film "Television film") such as *[Inside O.U.T.](/w/index.php?title=Inside_O.U.T.&action=edit&redlink=1 "Inside O.U.T. (page does not exist)")* (1971),[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerrace1985205-45) *[The Alpha Caper](/wiki/The_Alpha_Caper "The Alpha Caper")* (1973),[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKinney2012404-46) *[Calling Doctor Storm, M. D.](/w/index.php?title=Calling_Doctor_Storm,_M._D.&action=edit&redlink=1 "Calling Doctor Storm, M. D. (page does not exist)")* (1977),[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETerrace198572-47) *[Bay Cove](/w/index.php?title=Bay_Cove&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bay Cove (page does not exist)")* (1987),[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherman201521-48) *[Ollie Hopnoodle's Haven of Bliss](/wiki/Ollie_Hopnoodle%27s_Haven_of_Bliss "Ollie Hopnoodle's Haven of Bliss")* (1988),[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts200929-49) and *[Doing Time on Maple Drive](/wiki/Doing_Time_on_Maple_Drive "Doing Time on Maple Drive")* (1992).[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETropiano200298-50)[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2009426-51)

## Personal life and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_B._Sikking&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Personal life and death")]

Sikking married his second wife, cookbook author Florine Sikking ([née](/wiki/Birth_name#Maiden_and_married_names "Birth name") Caplan), in 1962.[[4]](#cite_note-Barnes-4) They had two children and four grandchildren.[[6]](#cite_note-Garner-6)[[8]](#cite_note-people-8) He and [Robert Wagner](/wiki/Robert_Wagner "Robert Wagner") were friends.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELambert2012311-52) Sikking enjoyed fishing. In the 1990s, he traveled to the [Florida Keys](/wiki/Florida_Keys "Florida Keys") to fish in the Redbone series of fishing tournaments.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpstein202141-53) Sikking lived in the [4500 block of Wilshire Blvd.](/wiki/Wilshire_Boulevard "Wilshire Boulevard") in Los Angeles.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETen-Tronck2005264-54)

Known affectionately as "Jim the Reader" at Los Angeles public schools, he read to public school third-grade classes for almost 20 years through the [Screen Actors Guild](/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild "Screen Actors Guild") (SAG) Book Pals Program.[[6]](#cite_note-Garner-6) Sikking did charity work for golf tournaments.[[7]](#cite_note-CST-7) He was at the ribbon-cutting ceremony for a health center in [Washington, Iowa](/wiki/Washington,_Iowa "Washington, Iowa").[[7]](#cite_note-CST-7)

Sikking died of complications from [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia") at his home in Los Angeles, on July 13, 2024, at age 90.[[4]](#cite_note-Barnes-4)[[6]](#cite_note-Garner-6)[[55]](#cite_note-Berman-55)[[56]](#cite_note-Carr-56)
